ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for sour and spicy sliced beef rice noodle specifically; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) as the most defensible ancestor available in the FoodOn ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based dish) used as the closest parent category; no specific code exists for suan cai fei niu mi xian in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific noodle dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of sour and spicy sliced beef rice noodle weighs approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as commonly served in Singapore Chinese noodle stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Sour & Spicy Sliced Beef Rice Noodle — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 酸菜肥牛米线 (Sour & Spicy Sliced Beef Rice Noodle), cr_id G00010505, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 1.6 g/100g based on the fatty acid profile of fatty beef (fei niu) as reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef, short loin, separable fat, raw (NDB 13413), proportioned to the fat content of this dish.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for suan cai fei niu mi xian as a composite dish. GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × available carbohydrate per serving (400 g × 25.19 g/100g × (1 − 3.7/25.19) = ~63.2 g net carb) / 100 ≈ 33.5. The protein and fat content of the beef and broth components will further moderate the postprandial glucose response below the calculated GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, which is 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — patients should request reduced broth volume and limit pickled mustard greens (suan cai) to control cumulative sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g; GI ~53 (low); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice noodles carry a GI of approximately 53 and a per-serving glycaemic load of ~33.5, which is high for a single meal; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and avoid sweetened broth additions to keep postprandial glucose within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from fatty beef (fei niu); sodium load from broth and pickled vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fatty beef (fei niu) contributes saturated fat (estimated ~1.6 g/100g) and the broth adds sodium; patients with cardiovascular disease should request lean beef substitution or reduced portion of beef, choose reduced-sodium broth, and limit this dish to occasional consumption within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid, omega-6) and chili/pickled nightshade-adjacent ingredients may amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sliced fatty beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine production relevant to RA; patients should consider substituting beef with chicken or tofu, request reduced chili and pickled mustard greens, and limit broth volume to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sour and Spicy Sliced Beef Rice Noodle (酸菜肥牛米线, suan cai fei niu mi xian) is a popular Chinese noodle dish widely served in Singapore's Chinese noodle stalls and casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of smooth, silky rice noodles (mi xian) submerged in a savoury, tangy beef broth, topped with thinly sliced fatty beef (fei niu) that cooks instantly in the hot soup, and generously garnished with pickled mustard greens (suan cai) that deliver the characteristic sour and mildly spicy flavour profile. Additional aromatics such as chili, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and spring onion round out the flavour. The texture contrast between the tender, melt-in-the-mouth beef slices, the soft yet slightly chewy rice noodles, and the crunchy pickled greens is a defining feature of the dish. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre, making it a moderate-energy, fibre-rich noodle dish. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, partially offset by a strong potassium content of 327 mg/100g. The glycaemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 53 (low category), though the per-serving glycaemic load is high (~33.5) due to portion size. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires sodium and portion management for patients with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The red meat content makes it unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients without ingredient substitution. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010505) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty beef data and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles, which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.6 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fatty beef (fei niu) proportioned to the dish fat content, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010505, and no equivalent composite-dish breakdown is available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026-04-16: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are set to -1 to indicate data not available from any approved source consulted; they are not zero and are not estimated. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB source, though fatty beef typically contains cholesterol — this may reflect the composite dish dilution effect or a reporting convention in the HPB database; downstream users should verify with HPB if cholesterol precision is clinically critical. 4. Halal status is marked false as this cannot be confirmed without vendor certification; the dish contains beef which is permissible under halal rules if certified, but certification is not confirmed. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the composite dish exists in approved sources. 6. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact term exists for this specific dish in either ontology catalogue.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Added explicit documentation of all -1 fields in the notes section with reason for unavailability and sources consulted, resolving G3 numeric hygiene failures.

Iteration 3: Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'] as required by G5 ontology honesty validation. Updated notes to document this correction.
